why-version-control-matters.label: Why a Project Needs History
why-version-control-matters.summary: Understand the problem Git solves before learning commands: one project, deliberate snapshots, and a history you can explain.

intro-memory.title: When “Final” Stops Meaning Final
intro-memory.bodyMarkdown:
A volunteer team is preparing a neighborhood guide. By Friday, the shared folder contains `guide-final.md`, `guide-final-fixed.md`, and `guide-final-really-final.md`. Each copy looks plausible, but nobody can explain which one contains the corrected phone number.

The problem is not a lack of files. It is a lack of history. A useful history answers three questions: **what changed, why did it change, and which state can the team trust now?**

Git keeps one project and records deliberate snapshots of it. You still work in ordinary files and folders. When a state is worth keeping, Git records that state with a message. Earlier snapshots remain available, so improving today’s version does not erase yesterday’s story.

worked-example.title: A Timeline You Can Read
worked-example.bodyMarkdown:
Imagine the guide begins with one page, gains an accessibility note, and later corrects an address. A readable history might look like this:

```text
Correct library address
Add accessibility note
Start neighborhood guide
```

The newest checkpoint appears first. Each line describes a result, not a button press or an automatic save. A maintainer can scan the timeline, choose a checkpoint, and inspect what changed there.

Git creates and reads this history locally. GitHub is a separate online service that can host Git repositories and support collaboration. This course stays local so the repository model is clear before remotes, accounts, or network workflows enter the picture.
